  • • Explanation

  • • Other Options:
  • • Other Options:

  • • Option A. Albumin
  • • Option B. Transthyretin : Incorrect as it binds less thyroid hormone than TBG, making it a secondary binder.
  • • Option B. Transthyretin
  • • Option D. All of the following bind equally : Incorrect because TBG binds a significantly higher percentage of thyroid hormones than the other proteins listed.
  • • Option D. All of the following bind equally

text: Which of the following is the correct sequence of muscles used in ejaculation? (FMGE AUGUST 2020)

opts.t: Ischiocavernosus contraction - External urethral sphincter relaxation- Bulbospongiosus contraction
Ischiocavernosus contraction – Bulbospongiosus contraction- external urethral sphincter relaxation
Ischiocavernosus relaxation - External urethral sphincter contraction- Bulbospongiosus relaxation
Bulbospongiosus contraction - Ischiocavernosus contraction -external urethral sphincter relaxation

exp:
Ans. B. Ischiocavernosus contraction – Bulbospongiosus contraction- external urethral sphincter relaxation

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • The sequences of events:
  • • Ejaculation involves a series of muscle contractions that expel seminal fluid from the prostatic urethra to the exterior, encompassing both autonomic and somatic control components. The correct sequence begins with the contraction of the ischiocavernosus muscle, which aids in the erection of the penis by compressing the base of the penile shaft and obstructing blood flow out of the erect penis, thereby maintaining its rigidity. Following this, the bulbospongiosus muscle contracts, which propels the seminal fluid along the urethra. The final step is the relaxation of the external urethral sphincter, which allows the semen to exit the urethra.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The correct sequence of muscular contractions during ejaculation: first, the ischiocavernosus muscle contracts to maintain erection, followed by the contraction of the bulbospongiosus muscle to propel seminal fluid, and finally, the relaxation of the external urethral sphincter allows the ejaculate to exit.

References:

  • ↳ Reference - Gray’s Anatomy- the Anatomical basis of clinical practice – 42 nd edition- page no. 1304

text: In seat belt injury, the most commonly involved organ is: (FMGE JANUARY 2024)
exp:
Ans. D) Hollow viscus

Explanation:

  • • Explanation
  • • Seat belts are known to decrease mortality and prevent head injuries in accidents. However, they can increase the risk of certain abdominal and thoracic injuries.
  • • Among all the thoracic and abdominal injuries associated with seat belt use, the maximum damage occurs to three organs: mesentery, omentum, and bowel (hollow viscus). This combination of injuries is known as the "seat belt syndrome." Therefore, the most commonly involved organ in seat belt injury is the hollow viscus.
  • • three organs: mesentery, omentum, and bowel (hollow viscus).
  • • Other Options:
  • • Other Options:
  • • Option. A. Liver + Spleen: Although liver and spleen injuries can occur in seat belt injuries, they are not the most commonly involved organs compared to the hollow viscus.
  • • Option. A. Liver + Spleen:
  • • Option. B. Lungs: While thoracic injuries can be increased due to seat belt use, the lungs are not the most commonly involved organ in seat belt injuries.
  • • Option. B. Lungs:
  • • Option. C. Head of Pancreas: The head of the pancreas is not the most commonly involved organ in seat belt injuries compared to the hollow viscus.
  • • Option. C. Head of Pancreas:

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ In seat belt injuries, the most commonly involved organ is the hollow viscus (bowel), along with the mesentery and omentum, constituting the "seat belt syndrome." Although seat belts are protective and prevent head injuries, they can increase the risk of certain abdominal and thoracic injuries, with the hollow viscus being the most frequently affected.

text: Which of the following is the method to prevent febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction? (FMGE JANUARY 2024)
exp:
Ans. C) Leucodepleted blood

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reactions (FNHTRs) are caused by antibodies in the recipient's plasma reacting with the donor's white blood cells (leukocytes) present in the transfused blood. The use of leucodepleted blood, which has been filtered to remove most of the leukocytes, significantly reduces the risk of FNHTRs by eliminating the trigger for the antibody reaction.
  • • Other Options:
  • • Other Options:
  • • Option A. Prophylactic paracetamol may help in managing fever if an FNHTR occurs, but it does not prevent the reaction itself.
  • • Option A. Prophylactic paracetamol
  • • Option B. Intravenous steroids are not typically used for the prevention of FNHTRs. Steroids may be administered in cases of severe transfusion reactions, but they do not specifically target the underlying cause of FNHTRs.
  • • Option B. Intravenous steroids
  • • Option D. Saline-washed red cell transfusion is a method used to reduce the risk of allergic reactions due to the presence of plasma proteins, but it does not directly address the issue of FNHTRs caused by leukocyte antibodies.
  • • Option D. Saline-washed red cell transfusion

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The use of leucodepleted blood, which has been filtered to remove most of the donor's white blood cells, is the recommended method to prevent febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reactions caused by antibody reactions against the transfused leukocytes.

text: In POP Q staging, the reference point taken is? (FMGE JANUARY 2024)
exp:
Ans. A) Hymen

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • The Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification (POP-Q) system is a standardized method for assessing and quantifying the severity of pelvic organ prolapse. The reference point in POP-Q staging is the hymen. All measurements in the POP-Q system are taken relative to the hymen, which serves as a fixed anatomical landmark.
  • • The POP-Q system involves six specific points and three measurements:
  • • Points:
  • • Points:
  • • Aa: A point on the anterior vaginal wall, 3 cm proximal to the hymen.
  • • Ba: The most distal position of any part of the anterior vaginal wall.
  • • C: The cervix or the vaginal cuff after hysterectomy.
  • • Ap: A point on the posterior vaginal wall, 3 cm proximal to the hymen.
  • • Bp: The most distal position of any part of the posterior vaginal wall.
  • • D: The posterior fornix or the deepest part of the vaginal vault.
  • • Measurements:
  • • Measurements:
  • • GH (Genital Hiatus): Distance from the external urethral meatus to the posterior hymen.
  • • PB (Perineal Body): Distance from the posterior hymen to the mid-anal opening.
  • • TVL (Total Vaginal Length): Distance from the hymen to the posterior fornix or vaginal apex.
  • • Other 3 Options: Not used as a reference point in POP-Q staging.
  • • Educational objective:
  • • Educational objective:
  • • In the POP-Q system, the hymen is used as the reference point for all measurements and staging of pelvic organ prolapse.

text: Which toxin can cause acute liver failure and hematuria? (FMGE JANUARY 2024)
exp:
Ans. D) Amanita phylloides

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Amanita phylloides, also known as the "death cap" mushroom, contains toxins that can cause both acute liver failure and hematuria. The mushroom contains amatoxins, which are responsible for the severe liver damage, and can also affect the kidneys, leading to renal damage and hematuria. This combination of liver and kidney damage is a characteristic feature of Amanita phylloides poisoning.
  • • Other options:
  • • Other options:
  • • Option. A. Morphine: Morphine is an opioid that can cause respiratory depression and constipation, but it is not typically associated with acute liver failure and hematuria.
  • • Option. A. Morphine:
  • • Option. B. Ketamine: Ketamine is an anesthetic that can cause dissociative effects and hallucinations, but it is not known to cause acute liver failure and hematuria.
  • • Option. B. Ketamine:
  • • Option. C. Belladonna: Atropa Belladonna, also known as deadly nightshade, contains anticholinergic compounds that can cause symptoms such as dry mouth, blurred vision, and tachycardia. However, it is not associated with acute liver failure and hematuria.
  • • Option. C. Belladonna:

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective
  • ➤ Amanita phylloides, or the "death cap" mushroom, is a toxin that can cause both acute liver failure and hematuria due to the presence of amatoxins. This combination of liver and kidney damage is a characteristic feature of Amanita phylloides poisoning

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • First-degree AV block is characterized by prolongation of the PR interval ( >0.2 seconds or >200 milliseconds ) with each P wave being followed by a QRS complex . This represents a delay in conduction through the AV node , but every atrial impulse still successfully conducts to the ventricles . On ECG , this appears as more than five small boxes between the P wave and QRS complex .

text: A person is exercising via performing different muscle movement, which of the following is correct regarding the muscle zone? (FMGE JAN 2025)
exp:
Ans. D) Band I decreases

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • During muscle contraction, the I band decreases in size due to increased overlapping between actin and myosin filaments. "When the muscle is going to start contracting, the overlapping between the actin and myosin is going to increase. And because of that, the I band (only actin) is going to decrease because more actin will be overlapped by the myosin."
  • • I band decreases in size due to increased overlapping between actin and myosin filaments.
  • • Other Options:
  • • Other Options:
  • • Option A. M-line decreases – Incorrect: The M line is the midline between two adjacent Z lines. "M line will never change" during muscle contraction. It remains constant as it's a structural component.
  • • Option A. M-line decreases – Incorrect:
  • • Option B. M-line & I band decreases - Incorrect : While I band does decrease, M line remains constant during contraction. Therefore, this combination is incorrect.
  • • Option B. M-line & I band decreases
  • • - Incorrect
  • • Option C. Band A decreases – Incorrect: A band represents the complete myosin filament length. "A band is a complete myosin that is nothing but whether it is overlapped by the actin or non-overlap does not matter. It will stay as it is." The A band length remains constant during contraction.
  • • Option C. Band A decreases – Incorrect:

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ During muscle contraction, while the M line and A band remain constant in length, the I band (containing only actin filaments) decreases in size due to increased actin-myosin overlap.

text: A female weighing 40 kg has a serum sodium level of 120 mEq/L. Calculate the sodium deficit for a desired sodium concentration of 140 mEq/L:
exp:
Ans. A) 400 mEq

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • The sodium deficit can be calculated using the formula:
  • • Sodium Deficit=Total Body Water×(Desired Sodium−Actual Sodium)
  • • For females, the Total Body Water (TBW) is 50% of body weight
  • • TBW=0.5×Body Weight
  • • Here, the body weight is 40 kg:
  • • TBW=0.5×40 kg=20 LTBW=0.5×40 kg=20 L
  • • The desired sodium concentration is 140 mEq/L and the actual sodium concentration is 120
  • • mEq/L:
  • • Sodium Deficit=20 L×(140 mEq/L−120 mEq/L) =400 mEq
  • • Therefore, the sodium deficit is 400 mEq.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objectives
  • ➤ The sodium deficit for a female weighing 40 kg with a serum sodium level of 120 mEq/L, aiming for a desired sodium concentration of 140 mEq/L, is 400 mEq.

text: What is the structure marked in the CT scan? (INCET MAY 2021)
exp:

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Anterior ethmoidal canal is noted by the marked arrows. The anterior ethmoidal canal can be recognized by the fact that it lies below the ethmoidal air cell roof. To prevent bleeding from this vessel during sinus procedures, it is crucial to recognize that the anterior ethmoidal artery runs via this canal. Maxillary sinus is noted in this view hence suggesting anterior plane.
  • • Other Options:
  • • Other Options:
  • • Option B. The posterior ethmoidal artery, vein, and nerve pass via the ethmoid bone’s posterior ethmoid canal or foramen. Due to the fact that this image is a coronal section taken at the level of the anterior ethmoidal cells, it cannot be seen.
  • • Option B.
  • • Option C. The optic nerve can be identified as an oval intraorbital structure in the coronal sections.
  • • Option C.
  • • Option D. The cribriform plate is a sieve-like structure between the nasal cavity and the anterior cranial fossa. It is part of the ethmoid bone and as it is pierced by the olfactory nerve fibers. Crista Galli is also a part of ethmoid bone that projects superiorly from the cribriform plate. It provides attachment for the falx cerebri.
  • • Option D.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The anterior ethmoidal canal is critical to identify during sinus procedures to avoid complications such as bleeding from the anterior ethmoidal artery. It is located below the roof of the ethmoidal air cells and anterior to the posterior ethmoidal canal.

exp:
Ans. D) Incontinence during intercourse

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ LUTS can be divided into three categories: storage, voiding, and post-micturitional.
  • ➤ Storage LUTS involves the failure of the bladder to act as a urine reservoir, leading to symptoms such as increased frequency of voiding, nocturia, urgency, and urge incontinence.
  • ➤ Voiding LUTS is commonly seen in bladder outlet obstruction and includes symptoms such as hesitancy, reduced stream, and straining.
  • ➤ Post-micturitional LUTS, also caused by bladder outlet obstruction, includes symptoms such as incomplete emptying and post-micturition dribble. Other symptoms of LUTS include stress incontinence, nocturnal enuresis, and strangury.

text: Identify the false statement out of the following: (INICET NOV 2021)

Lenvatinib is used for lesions less than 3 cm

NASH and NAFLD are risk factors for HCC

exp:
Ans. B) Lenvatinib is used for lesions less than 3 cm

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The Barcelona-Clinic-Liver Cancer (BCLC) Classification is the commonly used staging system for HCC. Very early HCC is treated by ablation, early HCC with a single nodule is resected, and when there are multiple nodules over 3 cm, ablation or transplantation are considered. Intermediate stage HCC is treated by chemoembolization, and TACE (Trans-arterial chemoembolization) is used for multinodular lesions. Advanced stages of the disease with macrovascular invasion are treated with systemic therapies. Lenvatinib and sorafenib are first-line drugs. Supportive care is given at the terminal stage of the disease.
  • ➤ The Barcelona-Clinic-Liver Cancer (BCLC) Classification is the commonly used staging system for HCC.
  • ➤ Very early HCC is treated by ablation, early HCC with a single nodule is resected, and when there are multiple nodules over 3 cm, ablation or transplantation are considered.
  • ➤ Intermediate stage HCC is treated by chemoembolization, and TACE (Trans-arterial chemoembolization) is used for multinodular lesions.
  • ➤ Advanced stages of the disease with macrovascular invasion are treated with systemic therapies. Lenvatinib and sorafenib are first-line drugs.
  • ➤ Supportive care is given at the terminal stage of the disease.

text: A young male weighing 46 kg was treated with a drug. It has a Vd of 6 L/kg and clearance of 4.6 L/hour. What is the approximate half-life of this drug? (INICET NOV 2024)
exp:
Ans. B) 40 hours

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • The half-life can be calculated using the formula:
  • • t1/2 = 0.693 × Vd/Clearance
  • • Vd given is 6 L/kg, so total Vd = 6 × 46 = 276 L Clearance = 4.6 L/hour Therefore: t1/2 = 0.693 × 276/4.6 ≈ 0.693 × 60 ≈ 40 hours
  • • Vd given is 6 L/kg, so total Vd = 6 × 46 = 276 L
  • • Clearance = 4.6 L/hour Therefore: t1/2 = 0.693 × 276/4.6 ≈ 0.693 × 60 ≈ 40 hours

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The formula for calculating half-life (t1/2 = 0.693 × Vd/Clearance) and remember to account for total Vd when given in L/kg by multiplying with body weight.

text: Which of the following should be avoided by a patient of fish odor syndrome?
exp:
Ans. B) Choline

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Fish odor syndrome (trimethylaminuria) is a metabolic syndrome caused by abnormal excretion of trimethylamine in the breath, urine, sweat, saliva and vaginal secretions . Trimethylamine is derived from the intestinal bacterial degradation of foods rich in choline and carnitine and is normally oxidized by the liver to odorless trimethylamine N-oxide which is then excreted in the urine. Impaired oxidation of trimethylamine due to the deficiency of flavin containing mono oxygenase 3 (FMO3 ) is thought to be the cause of the fish odor syndrome and is responsible for the smell of rotting fish.
  • ➤ caused by abnormal excretion of trimethylamine in the breath, urine, sweat, saliva and vaginal secretions
  • ➤ due to the deficiency of flavin containing mono oxygenase 3 (FMO3
  • ➤ Foods like eggs, liver, peanuts, meat, poultry, fish, dairy foods, pasta and rice are to be avoided.

text: Which of the following is true regarding an allosteric modifier? (NEET PG 2018)

opts.t: Causes the enzyme to work faster only

exp:
Ans. D) Participates in feedback regulation

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Allosteric Regulation
  • ➤ Allosteric Regulation
  • ➤ Allosteric enzyme has one catalytic site where the substrate binds and another separate allosteric site where the modifier binds (allo = other). Allosteric and substrate binding sites may or may not be physically adjacent. The binding of the regulatory molecule can either enhance the activity of the enzyme ( allosteric activation ), or inhibit the activity of the enzyme ( allosteric inhibition ). In the former case, the regulatory molecule is known as the positive modifier and in the latter case as the negative modifier . The binding of substrate to one of the subunits of the enzyme may enhance substrate binding by other subunits. This effect is said to be positive cooperativity . If the binding of substrate to one of the subunits decreases the avidity of substrate binding by other sites, the effect is called negative cooperativity. In feedback mechanism, one of the intermediates or products will go back and bind to the allosteric site of one of the enzymes involved in the series of reactions, thereby either inhibiting or activating the enzyme.
  • ➤ Allosteric enzyme has one catalytic site where the substrate binds and another separate allosteric site where the modifier binds (allo = other).
  • ➤ one catalytic site
  • ➤ separate allosteric site
  • ➤ Allosteric and substrate binding sites may or may not be physically adjacent.
  • ➤ The binding of the regulatory molecule can either enhance the activity of the enzyme ( allosteric activation ), or inhibit the activity of the enzyme ( allosteric inhibition ).
  • ➤ allosteric activation
  • ➤ allosteric inhibition
  • ➤ In the former case, the regulatory molecule is known as the positive modifier and in the latter case as the negative modifier .
  • ➤ positive modifier
  • ➤ negative modifier
  • ➤ The binding of substrate to one of the subunits of the enzyme may enhance substrate binding by other subunits. This effect is said to be positive cooperativity . If the binding of substrate to one of the subunits decreases the avidity of substrate binding by other sites, the effect is called negative cooperativity.
  • ➤ positive cooperativity
  • ➤ negative cooperativity.
  • ➤ In feedback mechanism, one of the intermediates or products will go back and bind to the allosteric site of one of the enzymes involved in the series of reactions, thereby either inhibiting or activating the enzyme.

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Seman's squeeze technique is a non-pharmacological method used to delay ejaculation in men with premature ejaculation. Here, when the man gets the feeling of impending ejaculation, the sex is stopped for some time and once excitement has decreased, it is restarted. Hence, it is also known as stop-start technique . Another non-pharmacological treatment includes squeeze technique , where the female partner squeezes the coronal ridge of the glans, when the man gets the feeling of impending ejaculation.
  • ➤ stop-start technique

text: C wave of JVP represents: (NEET PG 2020)

opts.t: Tricuspid bulge into right atrium during isovolumetric ventricular contraction

exp:

Explanation:

  • • Explanation
  • • 'C' wave in JVP represents tricuspid bulge into right atrium during isovolumetric ventricular contraction.
  • • Other Options :
  • • Other Options
  • • Option B. Contraction of right atrium : This is incorrect for the 'C' wave. The contraction of the right atrium produces the 'A' wave in the JVP, not the 'C' wave. The 'A' wave occurs due to atrial contraction pushing blood against a closed tricuspid valve at the end of diastole.
  • • Option B. Contraction of right atrium
  • • Option C. Passive filling of right atrium : Incorrect for the 'C' wave. Passive filling of the right atrium contributes to the 'V' wave, which occurs when the right atrium fills with blood during ventricular systole and the tricuspid valve is closed.
  • • Option C. Passive filling of right atrium
  • • Option D. Passive emptying of right atrium : Incorrect for the 'C' wave. Passive emptying of the right atrium is associated with the 'Y' descent, which happens when the tricuspid valve opens in early diastole and blood flows from the right atrium into the right ventricle.
  • • Option D. Passive emptying of right atrium
